html:
Going Blind

I met him in the Full-and-Plenty Dining Rooms. It was a cheap place in the city, with good beds upstairs let at one shilling per night‚Å†‚Äî‚ÄúBoard and residence for respectable single men, fifteen shillings per week.‚Äù I was a respectable single man then. I boarded and resided there. I boarded at a greasy little table in the greasy little corner under the fluffy little staircase in the hot and greasy little dining-room or restaurant downstairs. They called it dining-rooms, but it was only one room, and them wasn‚Äôt half enough room in it to work your elbows when the seven little tables and forty-nine chairs were occupied. There was not room for an ordinary-sized steward to pass up and down between the tables; but our waiter was not an ordinary-sized man‚Å†‚Äîhe was a living skeleton in miniature. We handed the soup, and the ‚Äúroast beef one,‚Äù and ‚Äúroast lamb one,‚Äù ‚Äúcorn beef and cabbage one,‚Äù ‚Äúveal and stuffing one,‚Äù and the ‚Äúveal and pickled pork,‚Äù one‚Å†‚Äîor two, or three, as the case might be‚Å†‚Äîand the tea and coffee, and the various kinds of puddings‚Å†‚Äîwe handed them over each other, and dodged the drops as well as we could. The very hot and very greasy little kitchen was adjacent, and it contained the bathroom and other conveniences, behind screens of whitewashed boards.

I resided upstairs in a room where there were five beds and one washstand; one candlestick, with a very short bit of soft yellow candle in it; the back of a hairbrush, with about a dozen bristles in it; and half a comb‚Å†‚Äîthe big-tooth end‚Å†‚Äîwith nine and a half teeth at irregular distances apart.

He was a typical bushman, not one of those tall, straight, wiry, brown men of the West, but from the old Selection Districts, where many drovers came from, and of the old bush school; one of those slight active little fellows whom we used to see in cabbage-tree hats, Crimean shirts, strapped trousers, and elastic-side boots‚Å†‚Äî‚Äúlarstins,‚Äù they called them. They could dance well; sing indifferently, and mostly through their noses, the old bush songs; play the concertina horribly; and ride like‚Å†‚Äîlike‚Å†‚Äîwell, they could ride.

He seemed as if he had forgotten to grow old and die out with this old colonial school to which he belonged. They had careless and forgetful ways about them. His name was Jack Gunther, he said, and he‚Äôd come to Sydney to try to get something done to his eyes. He had a portmanteau, a carpet bag, some things in a three-bushel bag, and a tin bog. I sat beside him on his bed, and struck up an acquaintance, and he told me all about it. First he asked me would I mind shifting round to the other side, as he was rather deaf in that ear. He‚Äôd been kicked by a horse, he said, and had been a little dull o‚Äô hearing on that side ever since.

He was as good as blind. ‚ÄúI can see the people near me,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúbut I can‚Äôt make out their faces. I can just make out the pavement and the houses close at hand, and all the rest is a sort of white blur.‚Äù He looked up: ‚ÄúThat ceiling is a kind of white, ain‚Äôt it? And this,‚Äù tapping the wall and putting his nose close to it, ‚Äúis a sort of green, ain‚Äôt it?‚Äù The ceiling might have been whiter. The prevalent tints of the wallpaper had originally been blue and red, but it was mostly green enough now‚Å†‚Äîa damp, rotten green; but I was ready to swear that the ceiling was snow and that the walls were as green as grass if it would have made him feel more comfortable. His sight began to get bad about six years before, he said; he didn‚Äôt take much notice of it at first, and then he saw a quack, who made his eyes worse. He had already the manner of the blind‚Å†‚Äîthe touch of every finger, and even the gentleness in his speech. He had a boy down with him‚Å†‚Äîa ‚Äúsorter cousin of his,‚Äù and the boy saw him round. ‚ÄúI‚Äôll have to be sending that youngster back,‚Äù he said, ‚ÄúI think I‚Äôll send him home next week. He‚Äôll be picking up and learning too much down here.‚Äù

I happened to know the district he came from, and we would sit by the hour and talk about the country, and chaps by the name of this and chaps by the name of that‚Å†‚Äîdrovers mostly, whom we had met or had heard of. He asked me if I‚Äôd ever heard of a chap by the name of Joe Scott‚Å†‚Äîa big sandy-complexioned chap, who might be droving; he was his brother, or, at least, his half-brother, but he hadn‚Äôt heard of him for years; he‚Äôd last heard of him at Blackall, in Queensland; he might have gone overland to Western Australia with Tyson‚Äôs cattle to the new country.

We talked about grubbing and fencing and digging and droving and shearing‚Å†‚Äîall about the bush‚Å†‚Äîand it all came back to me as we talked. ‚ÄúI can see it all now,‚Äù he said once, in an abstracted tone, seeming to fix his helpless eyes on the wall opposite. But he didn‚Äôt see the dirty blind wall, nor the dingy window, nor the skimpy little bed, nor the greasy washstand; he saw the dark blue ridges in the sunlight, the grassy sidings and flats, the creek with clumps of sheoak here and there, the course of the willow-fringed river below, the distant peaks and ranges fading away into a lighter azure, the granite ridge in the middle distance, and the rocky rises, the stringy-bark and the apple-tree flats, the scrubs, and the sunlit plains‚Å†‚Äîand all. I could see it, too‚Å†‚Äîplainer than ever I did.

He had done a bit of fencing in his time, and we got talking about timber. He didn‚Äôt believe in having fencing-posts with big butts; he reckoned it was a mistake. ‚ÄúYou see,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúthe top of the butt catches the rain water and makes the post rot quicker. I‚Äôd back posts without any butt at all to last as long or longer than posts with ‚Äôem‚Å†‚Äîthat‚Äôs if the fence is well put up and well rammed.‚Äù He had supplied fencing stuff, and fenced by contract, and‚Å†‚Äîwell, you can get more posts without butts out of a tree than posts with them. He also objected to charring the butts. He said it only made more work‚Å†‚Äîand wasted time‚Å†‚Äîthe butts lasted longer without being charred.

I asked him if he‚Äôd ever got stringy-bark palings or shingles out of mountain ash, and he smiled a smile that did my heart good to see, and said he had. He had also got them out of various other kinds of trees.

We talked about soil and grass, and gold-digging, and many other things which came back to one like a revelation as we yarned.

He had been to the hospital several times. ‚ÄúThe doctors don‚Äôt say they can cure me,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúthey say they might, be able to improve my sight and hearing, but it would take a long time‚Å†‚Äîanyway, the treatment would improve my general health. They know what‚Äôs the matter with my eyes,‚Äù and he explained it as well as he could. ‚ÄúI wish I‚Äôd seen a good doctor when my eyes first began to get weak; but young chaps are always careless over things. It‚Äôs harder to get cured of anything when you‚Äôre done growing.‚Äù

He was always hopeful and cheerful. ‚ÄúIf the worst comes to the worst,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúthere‚Äôs things I can do where I come from. I might do a bit o‚Äô wool-sorting, for instance. I‚Äôm a pretty fair expert. Or else when they‚Äôre weeding out I could help. I‚Äôd just have to sit down and they‚Äôd bring the sheep to me, and I‚Äôd feel the wool and tell them what it was‚Å†‚Äîbeing blind improves the feeling, you know.‚Äù

He had a packet of portraits, but he couldn‚Äôt make them out very well now. They were sort of blurred to him, but I described them and he told me who they were. ‚ÄúThat‚Äôs a girl o‚Äô mine,‚Äù he said, with reference to one‚Å†‚Äîa jolly, good-looking bush girl. ‚ÄúI got a letter from her yesterday. I managed to scribble something, but I‚Äôll get you, if you don‚Äôt mind, to write something more I want to put in on another piece of paper, and address an envelope for me.‚Äù

Darkness fell quickly upon him now‚Å†‚Äîor, rather, the ‚Äúsort of white blur‚Äù increased and closed in. But his hearing was better, he said, and he was glad of that and still cheerful. I thought it natural that his hearing should improve as he went blind.

One day he said that he did not think he would bother going to the hospital any more. He reckoned he‚Äôd get back to where he was known. He‚Äôd stayed down too long already, and the ‚Äústuff‚Äù wouldn‚Äôt stand it. He was expecting a letter that didn‚Äôt come. I was away for a couple of days, and when I came back he had been shifted out of the room and had a bed in an angle of the landing on top of the staircase, with the people brushing against him and stumbling over his things all day on their way up and down. I felt indignant, thinking that‚Å†‚Äîthe house being full‚Å†‚Äîthe boss had taken advantage of the bushman‚Äôs helplessness and good nature to put him there. But he said that he was quite comfortable. ‚ÄúI can get a whiff of air here,‚Äù he said.

Going in next day I thought for a moment that I had dropped suddenly back into the past and into a bush dance, for there was a concertina going upstairs. He was sitting on the bed, with his legs crossed, and a new cheap concertina on his knee, and his eyes turned to the patch of ceiling as if it were a piece of music and he could read it. ‚ÄúI‚Äôm trying to knock a few tunes into my head,‚Äù he said, with a brave smile, ‚Äúin case the worst comes to the worst.‚Äù He tried to be cheerful, but seemed worried and anxious. The letter hadn‚Äôt come. I thought of the many blind musicians in Sydney, and I thought of the bushman‚Äôs chance, standing at a corner swanking a cheap concertina, and I felt sorry for him.

I went out with a vague idea of seeing someone about the matter, and getting something done for the bushman‚Å†‚Äîof bringing a little influence to his assistance; but I suddenly remembered that my clothes were worn out, my hat in a shocking state, my boots burst, and that I owed for a week‚Äôs board and lodging, and was likely to be thrown out at any moment myself; and so I was not in a position to go where there was influence.

When I went back to the restaurant there was a long, gaunt sandy-complexioned bushman sitting by Jack‚Äôs side. Jack introduced him as his brother, who had returned unexpectedly to his native district, and had followed him to Sydney. The brother was rather short with me at first, and seemed to regard the restaurant people‚Å†‚Äîall of us, in fact‚Å†‚Äîin the light of spielers who wouldn‚Äôt hesitate to take advantage of Jack‚Äôs blindness if he left him a moment; and he looked ready to knock down the first man who stumbled against Jack, or over his luggage‚Å†‚Äîbut that soon wore off. Jack was going to stay with Joe at the Coffee Palace for a few weeks, and then go back upcountry, he told me. He was excited and happy. His brother‚Äôs manner towards him was as if Jack had just lost his wife, or boy or someone very dear to him. He would not allow him to do anything for himself, nor try to‚Å†‚Äînot even lace up his boot. He seemed to think that he was thoroughly helpless, and when I saw him pack up Jack‚Äôs things, and help him at the table and fix his tie and collar with his great brown hands, which trembled all the time with grief and gentleness, and make Jack sit down on the bed whilst he got a cab and carried the trap down to it, and take him downstairs as if he were made of thin glass, and settle with the landlord‚Å†‚Äîthen I knew that Jack was all right.

We had a drink together‚Å†‚ÄîJoe, Jack, the cabman, and I. Joe was very careful to hand Jack the glass, and Jack made joke about it for Joe‚Äôs benefit. He swore he could see a glass yet, and Joe laughed, but looked extra troubled the next moment.

I felt their grips on my hand for five minutes after we parted.
